caption.text: Throughout the last century, East Boston has been the home of numerous city housing, development, and neighborhood revitalization projects. Before the first public housing, Maverick Gardens, was developed in the early 1940s, East Boston was one of the city’s key industrial sites.
caption.title: Introduction

caption.text: In 1940, Boston [gained the funding](https://www.proquest.com/docview/816891146/E2548D8D3BB647BEPQ/4?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) to begin work on a housing project in East Boston in Maverick Square. The project would be located between New St., Maverick St., Havre St., and Sumner St. Its primary aim would be to house the families of soldiers fighting in World War II, but would [also include](https://www.proquest.com/docview/839320215/E2548D8D3BB647BEPQ/6?sourcetype=Newspapers) some general low income housing..

The housing project looked very different from the homes there before. Previously, [as shown in this newspaper clip](https://www.proquest.com/docview/816891146/E2548D8D3BB647BEPQ/4?sourcetype=Newspapers), the buildings ranged in appearance and height, crafted seemingly of wood, some having porches and others having gabled roofs.

All of the buildings in the new project were three stories high, made of brick, and exactly uniform, spanning several blocks. These buildings stuck out in their surroundings, and some of the previous individuality and neighborhood charm was lost in new redevelopment.

How did East Bostonians earn a living? This map shows that industry on the waterfront was already prevalent here, serving as a shipyard for imports and the start of the railroad to ship the products across the state. By 1922, just 20 years before the public housing would begin construction, East Boston was already booming with industries like coal, wool, electricity, and storage.

East Boston residents had ample employment opportunites right on their doorstep. This photo depicts Sumner Street before the public housing development. On the right side, the many buildings lining the street housed families and working class Bostonians.
Just across the street sits Staples Coal Company...
...and just a block away at the intersection of New Street is Boston Cold Storage Company.
The neighborhood blended together with the factories and plants, meaning that these residents likely lived here for their proximity to work.
